papers.title: Influence of a stellar cusp on the dynamics of young stellar discs and the origin of the S-stars in the Galactic Centre
papers.author_names: U. Löckmann H. Baumgardt P. Kroupa

abstract.text: Observations of the Galactic Centre show evidence of one or two disc-like structures of very young stars orbiting the central super-massive black hole within a distance of a few 0.1 pc. A number of analyses have been carried out to investigate the dynamical behaviour and consequences of these discs, including disc thickness and eccentricity growth as well as mutual interaction and warping. However, most of these studies have neglected the influence of the stellar cusp surrounding the black hole, which is believed to be 1-2 orders of magnitude more massive than the disc(s).\\\n  By means of N-body integrations using our bhint code, we study the impact of stellar cusps of different compositions. We find that although the presence of a cusp does have an important effect on the evolution of an otherwise isolated flat disc, its influence on the evolution of disc thickness and warping is rather mild in a two-disc configuration. However, we show that the creation of highly eccentric orbits strongly depends on the graininess of the cusp (i.e. the mean and maximum stellar masses): While Chang (2009) recently found that full cycles of Kozai resonance are prevented by the presence of an analytic cusp, we show that relaxation processes play an important role in such highly dense regions and support short-term resonances. We thus find that young disc stars on initially circular orbits can achieve high eccentricities by resonant effects also in the presence of a cusp of stellar remnants, yielding a mechanism to create S-stars and hyper-velocity stars.\\\n  Furthermore, we discuss the underlying initial mass function (IMF) of the young stellar discs and find no definite evidence for a non-canonical IMF.

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Results: The stellar cusp significantly enhances disc warping and eccentricity evolution, particularly for counter-clockwise stars, but cannot fully explain the observed high eccentricities and inclinations of the S-stars without additional perturbations such as a second disc or massive black hole. Conclusions: The stellar cusp in the Galactic Centre drives the evolution of young stellar discs by enhancing orbital eccentricities and disc thickness through non-resonant relaxation, leading to binary disruption and S-star formation, while also enabling detectable stellar black hole–supermassive black hole mergers. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of incorporating realistic stellar cusp potentials—both analytic and N-body—into dynamical models of young stellar discs around the Galactic Centre, thereby addressing the problem of how such cusps influence disc warping, eccentricity evolution, and the formation of observed S-stars and hyper-velocity stars, which previous studies neglected. Numerical method and models: The paper proposes a high-precision $N$-body integration method using the bhint code that solves Kepler’s equation analytically to accurately model stellar disc dynamics around a supermassive black hole, incorporating general relativistic post-Newtonian effects only for highly eccentric orbits where they are significant. Structure of the stellar cusp in the Galactic Centre: The stellar cusp at the Galactic Centre exhibits a power-law density profile with γ ≈ 1.75 beyond 0.22 pc, dominating the dynamics of young stellar discs and serving as the primary perturbation to their evolution, despite the presence of other structures like the circum-nuclear disc.
